sentences.text: I see.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
We were at the -- [INAUDIBLE] we were at the-- 206 and 208.
Yes.
Yes.

One thing which I did not mention, which I forgot now to-- to put in and which is very, very important-- I mean, for me it's extremely important-- that before they put me to the appellplatz, these two women-- because they wanted to get rid of me-- they actually tried to bribe me.
The way they tried to bribe me, that they first-- after they found out that I knew German, they came to me and they offered me to become work leader.
And I-- now, to become work leader would have been a wonderful thing because you had much more food, you had easier life.
It would have been wonderful.
I refused it, because I said that I wanted to survive, but I did not want to survive any price.
And, therefore, I said I am not prepared to push other people around, and I don't want to be work leader.
And so I-- I refused it, and then the next day they came and put me into this [INAUDIBLE] for the selection, for these 206.
But once they saw that I was sent back, they didn't do anything about it.
They left me in peace, and that was over with.
That saved me.
Burying the dog or whatever saved my life because these 206 were sent back to Auschwitz and were never ever heard of again.
That was the end of them.
They weren't sent to a paper factory, as they were told.
And so this-- this was one story which was very important for me because I always felt I want to say that one did have a choice.
There was a choice, at certain points.
Obviously, you risked your life, but there was a choice.
And I wanted to survive, but not at any price, and that is a point which I very much wanted to make.
Because I lived according to that during my whole time in camp, except for that one incident when I was really vicious and nasty and pushed out that one person.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, we were-- I just want to describe very quickly the inside of-- I think I told already.
In the barracks we had to these bunks, and we had one prickly, straw-filled mattress.
And we also had a prickly blanket, individual blanket.
One for each of us, which was luxury, in spite of it that they were prickly.
And in August the central heating was on because there was central heating there.
And the central heating was on in August.
It wasn't on in winter, which wasn't so good.
That was one thing about the barracks.
Also was a very long table, and people did all kinds of things in there.

Now I have no-- haven't found yet any corroboration-- now, I find that there are certain stories which some people tell and others don't tell-- but I found out that it must be because we were working in shifts, and certain things others didn't.
And I didn't come across anybody who was in my shift and who remembered one of these odd stories.
For three days we very given-- either three days-- for-- for three days we were given three marks each.
Or four days, I don't remember.
Three or four days, as-- as a payment.
Suddenly they said they are going to pay us.
And then we didn't get any more paid, so we had our nine-- three days, because we had our nine mark.
And then they opened a shop.
And in that shop you could buy two things.
You could buy either an envelope, a sheet of paper, and borrow a pencil to write to Auschwitz to your family.
Which, of course, was absolutely ridiculous, but many people did it because they didn't believe that their people weren't in Auschwitz anymore.
Or you could buy a lipstick.
And I don't remember anymore.
I know that I didn't buy the lipstick.
I think I did buy a paper so as to have paper, a sheet of paper.
I didn't send it off because there was no point of sending it off.
But I did buy-- I didn't buy lipstick.
The women went mad and were killing each other for the lipstick.
There weren't enough lipsticks to go around, and they weren't the right colors.
So if anybody found the right colors, they were killing each other.

Now, at that time to me it was completely ludicrous, and the German's were laughing their heads off about the stupid women who are starving, who look emaciated, who look terrible, who are in clothes which don't fit them, and yet they-- they kill each other for a lipstick.
And I didn't understand it, but today I think I understand it.
They had to somehow-- I was never vain, so I couldn't have understood at the time at all, because I didn't use a lipstick.
I refused to use lipstick or powder or anything because I always said I must be loved for myself, not how I make myself up.
I mustn't put on a mask.
But-- but today I understand it.
These people, they somehow had to-- had to show that they were women, had to prove to themselves that they were women.
But I still couldn't understand how they could fight about it.
And that was every time we had clothes given there was such a fight going on.
I-- I never went there.
I always went there at the last one and what was on the floor I picked it up and put it on.
And I looked like a scarecrow.
Everybody remembers me looking like a scarecrow because I always had on something odd which nobody wanted anymore.
But that-- that--
So there is so much I think about camp.
Now, because Willy saved my life, I-- I was-- had this ambivalent feeling with him.
I-- I-- I didn't hate him.
I didn't love him, but I didn't hate him.
I-- I wanted to put that down because I-- it will come back in another way later.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
We were brought there to work in a factory.
We were working in the second-largest munitions factory that was in Germany.
It was a factory by Dynamit Nobel AG.
And, oddly enough, it was-- in these days it was in where we are talking about is not by name.
The man was Frick, and I don't know whether the name Frick means anything to you, but Frick was the one who owned this factory.
And-- and this factory produced-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because-- because Frick later on refused to pay anything to the slave laborers.
Anyway, this-- this factory produced grenades and mines.
Now, I was put on the grenade production, and I was-- and we had a group of people, a group of women, who did not quite agree of helping the German war effort.
So we formed a sabotage group.
That is to say, I exaggerate when I say 'we formed.' But I have been asked to be a part, take-- take part in a sabotage group.
I only knew the one before me.
I didn't know who else was in the chain.
And I must say even that I forgot.
I don't know got me into there.
But obviously they thought I was a reliable.

And what happened, there were quite a few chemists amongst us, and they managed to get in to the so-called 'filling rooms,' where they prepared the mixture for the grenades and filled the-- the hulls with the mixture.
And, of course, because they were chemists and knew how to change the quantities so that these mixtures shouldn't work and shouldn't be-- shouldn't explode.
They increased the production by threefold.
Now, before us there were German workers working in the factory.
They increased the production to threefold what the German workers did.
Everybody complained.
All the other women complained.
We worked together with Germans, and they complained because they never had to work so hard because it didn't-- the production never moved so hard.
But they increased the production threefold.
And the-- the foreman-- foreman, the German foreman, saw that the stupid Jewish women are so frightened that they work so hard.
So the control became very superficial.
And because-- and-- and that's what we aimed at.
Because through that, in the filling room, they could do with the mixture whatever they wanted, and they could change the mixture so that it couldn't explode.

If there was control in the room, they made a certain sign on the grenade which we knew, we who took part, the few people who took part in the sabotage group knew, and then we could-- we could do something else.
We had to do something else to ruin the grenade.
But they started it off.
And I was on a conveyor belt doing with some pliers putting on a Bakelite cap and then twisting it.
I had to reinforce it with pliers.
Now, I made sure that I make the movement, but I didn't put it on properly.
I first of all tried to put it on slant so that I shouldn't be able to screw it properly.
And I made sure that I didn't.
But I felt that I was going mad on the conveyor belt, and so I go out, and at the first occasion when they asked for a volunteer to work outside.
It was-- the job was called a horse.
And I volunteered.
And from then I became a horse.
My job was when the grenades came off the conveyor belt, I had to put them onto a flat iron lorry, very heavy, iron lorry which was on wheels.
And I had to put on two grenades, and each one weighed 25 kilogram.
At the same time, I had to load it up with 14 grenades in a way that the grenades shouldn't roll off, because it was very dangerous.
One of my toes was squashed like that, and actually one of my fingers was also hit by a rolling-down grenade.
So I had to be very careful not to.
And then I had to roll out the trolley from this bay, from the bay where-- where they-- the belt was, the conveyor belt was, and take it through a heavy iron door which opened outward.
And I had to do it very fast so that no light should go out, especially at night, no light.
In the dark, no light should go out because of air raids.
And then put it on rails and let it down on the rails a very steep hill.

And at the end of the steep hill, stop it, jump aside, stop it, and-- and open up the offloading bays, iron door-- heavy iron door, which opened outward, again being careful that the light shouldn't come on.
Roll it in and then offload it.

And at the stage where I offloaded them, I always offloaded them to trying-- to-- to-- in a way that I try to break the Bakelite cap and-- and-- so that I was pretty sure that any grenades that went out from my hands-- through my hands-- did not explode.
Now, we were never found out, but had we been found out, obviously we would have been shot on the spot.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] during the times that I let the trolley down this very steep hill on the rails, I always had someone to work the brakes.
And for a while I had a German worker working the brakes.
Now, this German worker had very gruff manner.
For some reason or other, I knew-- we were not allowed to speak, even though we were outside.
I mean, it was all outside, that it was-- but we were not allowed to speak.
And he had a very gruff manner.
If he had to give me an order or something, was very gruff always.
But he was marvelous, marvelous in-- in the brakes.
I was always happy if he was on because if anybody else was on I wasn't sure whether I'm going to be crushed against this iron door or not.
But with him I could be absolutely sure because he was wonderful.
And from time to time he would come back-- we heard that he was a man who had seven children and was very poor.

And from time to time he would come, and in his very gruff manner, would put into my hand a piece of newspaper where there was a tiny little onion in it or a tiny little piece of bread and would tell me, you go into that corner and eat it there, but give me back the paper.
There was always something on the paper that warmed my heart.
Some little defeat or some victory or-- or something which the-- I-- and I was immensely grateful to him.
He then suddenly disappeared.
There were rumors that he did help one of our inmates and he was sent away to Westphalia, to where they were-- on to the front.
I don't know.
But, anyway, I never-- and I'm very, very sorry I could never find out his name, because I would have loved to thank him for his kindness.
Because it was really a piece of humanity in those-- in those days, for which I was very grateful.
Now, down in the offloading bay there was a Frenchman and a Ukrainian girl who were working.
And I picked up a few actually I believe Russian words and not Ukrainian words.
I don't know.
Perhaps she was a Russian girl.
I thought she was Ukrainian, and I-- And the Frenchman used to bring me news.
Somehow the man had always news, and he used to bring me news so I could bring them back the news.
We always had a few minutes.
Most of time there was no control, and we had a few minutes.
While I was offloading we could speak to each other.
We're not supposed to, but we could speak to each other since I spoke French, so it was all right.
Now, we-- we were getting weaker and weaker.

And what I did not say is that we had-- when we arrived in Hessich-Lichtenau we got a big piece of cloth about this size, white piece of cloth, on which our-- each of our number was printed on in very, very large letters.
And we had to sew that onto our clothing at the back.
So we were numbers.
From that moment on we were numbers, except that I became an individual to the commander because I buried his dog, which was a fantastic thing, I mean, to become and individual again.
But we were numbers, and anybody who disliked you or some things that you did could denounce you through your number.
And everybody could see from far.
We walked to work through two villages.
Most of the time we walked.
Sometimes we went by train, but most of the time we walked through two villages so everybody could see us with our shaven head, barefoot in winter, in snow and ice, and even before that.
So that when people tell me they didn't know anything, I-- I get very angry because they did know it.
They couldn't overlook us.
We were in the middle of the town, and they couldn't ignore us.
There was no way that they could ignore us, and we were getting more and more emaciated as time went on.
On the end of March-- from December onward, we heard American guns.
And again I did my bit with my ESP.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody was thrilled that the Americans were coming and we would be liberated.
It was before Christmas.
We would be liberated soon.
And I suddenly came up and I said no.
We are not going to be liberated before the 23rd of April, which happened to be my birthday.
I said I'm not sure that I will be around.
I probably won't be around.
But this group won't be liberated before the 23rd of April.
And we weren't.
We weren't.
But how did I know?
I don't know, but I did.
Again they were very angry with me and-- and-- and that I was always spreading bad news and I was always taking everybody's pleasure away and how did I know anyway?
And I'm a liar.
And I'm a-- But, anyway, that's-- that's what happened.
And I was adamant that I knew it.
Why?
I don't know.
Then I-- at the end of March we were-- we were-- we had to leave camp.
And we went to-- we were put into trains.
And by that time the Americans were around and the train was followed by American planes.
And on the-- I don't know.
Very-- very soon, I don't know, second day or something like that, the Americans dive-bombed the engine and so we couldn't go further.
And we were sitting on the line for the next three days.
Rumors were, or we were told-- I don't remember anymore-- that we were going to be taken to Buchenwald, to the main camp, where we would have been exterminated.
But we weren't-- then we were three days on the line.
And by the time they got another engine, they couldn't-- they didn't take us to Buchenwald anymore, but they took us to Leipzig.
And in Leipzig they took us to a camp which was called Schonau, which was a very nice camp.
There was already another group of Hungarians there who were very nicely dressed, very well fed, who had a commandant who looked after them.
Didn't let them work very hard.
Fed them during the whole time and really looked after them and was really wonderful to them.
And he was now the commandant of this camp for-- for-- we didn't know for how long.
And there were beautiful showers and we had showers and we had a light meal and we got clean clothes.
And which-- And then in the afternoon we were resting on our bunks, on our very clean, beautiful bunks, and suddenly they started-- the Americans started bombarding our camp.
And we laid down our heads.
My hand-- the hand of a friend of mine and she got killed.
And quite a few got killed.
And the camp burned down.
And then they took us through Leipzig, walked us through Leipzig, which was burning because the biggest bombardment was the 7th of-- of April, 1945.
And they took us there-- took us to another camp, which was called Thekla.
Now, Thekla was a camp, must have been a very large camp, because there were already lots of men there from Buchenwald.
We were separated from them by wire and we were not supposed to speak to them, but we still managed to speak to them.
And I-- I remained in a flannel-- sleeveless, flannel shirt.
It was still snowing.
Barefoot, sleeveless, flannel shirt.
That was all that I had on.
And one of the Frenchmen gave me one of his jackets.
It was rather threadbare, but at least covered me a little bit.
He also gave me a piece of soap and we spoke a few times.
And I don't remember how many days we were there exactly.
But I know-- well, it would have been six days, I think so, because I know that we-- on the 13th we were put onto the road-- 12th or 13th we were put onto the road for our death march.
And for the next ten days we were on our death march.
We didn't have anything.
We were 15,000 men and women, and at every stop this man who gave me my jacket and my soap would come up, and he was weaker and weaker.
And after about four days he disappeared.
He didn't come anymore.
I didn't see him anymore.
This is a very important point.
And then he-- then we went-- we dragged ourselves on.
And some people managed to escape when we got to a forest.
Luciana and four other friends of mine, my closest friends, escaped.
And then we heard shots from the forest and the SS said, well, they have been found and they have been shot, which wasn't true, luckily.
But I-- I dragged on.
And-- And I dragged on.
And we were taken to-- they told us they are taking us towards Dresden and over the Elba.
And they took us over the Elba and that-- and then somehow in a circle.
We went round and around in a circle for 10 days.
I don't know how many bridges we crossed or the same bridges.
I don't remember anymore.
I only know that on the tenth day we were camped on the other side of the Elba, because by that time it was already clear.
On this side were the Americans.
And, by the way, during the death march the German guards put on striped prison clothing so that they shouldn't be seen as separate.
And the Americans were low flying, so low flying that we waved to them constantly.
And they were accompanying us the whole way.
And when we went over the Elba, then there were Russians on the other side.
And they were low flying.
And we were in a forest next to the Elba.
And that night we camped in a clearing in the forest and they slaughtered a horse and gave us pieces of raw meat, raw horse meat, and also and a handful of raw rice.
And we ate it.
Again we had dysentery.
We were ill.
We were weak.
And during this death march, anybody who collapsed, anybody-- and there were very many-- they were shot on the spot and left there.
And we were always less and less who walked on, who carried on.
But we carried on.
And after that night the Russians circulated with low flying airplanes, circulated us.
And then in the morning, early in the morning, the guards woke us up and they said we are going back on the same bridge on which we came.
And we went.
And it was dark when we started, and as we walked there was a sunrise.
And it was an incredible sunrise.
I was incredibly, very, very, very weak.
I hardly could drag myself.
But it was the most beautiful sunrise I ever saw.
And as we got over the bridge, I collapsed, but I had-- I didn't mind because I had this beautiful sunrise and I thought, well, now I can die.
It doesn't matter anymore.
And I collapsed.
And two SS came to me and started shouting to me to get up.
I couldn't.
I had dysentery and I was altogether very weak.
I couldn't.
And then they butted me with their guns and then one of them said, Leave her.
She's not worth a bullet anymore.
And they walked off to chase those who still could walk further.
And so I was left there, and I really couldn't get up.
I saw a farmhouse not far away, and I started to crawl on my stomach really, drag myself on the stomach.
And I got into a stable, got myself into a stable.
At least I was warm there with the animals.
And as I was sitting there, a German girl came in, a young girl.
And she saw me there and she went out again, didn't say anything.
Went out again, came back with a piece of bread.
And the she told me, don't stay here because there are still some German soldiers out here.
They might come in to take the horses.
But try and get there.
She showed me a barn.
So I crawled again somehow, as I say, not even crawled but really on my stomach.
I heave myself and got myself after quite a long time into this barn and hid there.
There were already two of my camp mates who already hid-- were hiding there.
And we were there-- and then it got evening.
And in the evening-- it was dark.
Suddenly the door opened and in came five men.
And one had a torch and the other one had a knife and the other one had a revolver.
And they were all armed.
And they started looking and they found us.
